known_systematic_differences: CaCl2: app uses dihydrate (Palmer 2013), Bru'n uses anhydrous. ~33% Cl difference on CaCl2-only batches. Deliberate choice.
Baking soda Alk: 9 ppm low vs Bru'n in Batch 2. Open investigation item — possibly Bru'n's CO2 outgassing model.

name: Batch 1 — Chemistry parity, Bristlecone water + typical pale ale salt mix
notes: Hand-picked salt amounts via Bru'n's calculator. Tests chemistry engine ion contribution constants for Ca, SO4, Mg, Cl, plus 88% lactic acid math. NOTE: CaCl2 constants use dihydrate (CaCl2*2H2O, MW 147.01) per Palmer & Kaminski (2013) p. 282 — homebrewing convention. Bru'n Water uses anhydrous CaCl2, so Cl values disagree by ~33% on CaCl2-only batches. This is a deliberate choice, not a bug. Bru'n input HCO3 = source.Alkalinity × 1.22 = 61 ppm. Bru'n output HCO3 converted back to Alk-as-CaCO3 (× 0.82) for brun_predicted_final_ions.Alk.

name: Batch 2 — Chemistry parity, Bristlecone + alkalinity-raising salts
notes: Hand-picked salt amounts via Bru'n's calculator. Tests chemistry engine ion contribution constants for baking soda (NaHCO3 → Na + HCO3 → Alk-as-CaCO3). This validates the alkalinity-raising path that Batch 1 doesn't exercise. Bru'n input HCO3 = source.Alkalinity × 1.22 = 61 ppm. Bru'n output HCO3 converted back to Alk-as-CaCO3 (× 0.82) for brun_predicted_final_ions.Alk.

name: Batch 3 — Chemistry parity, NaCl + MgCl2 + gypsum
notes: Hand-picked salt amounts via Bru'n's calculator. Tests Na from NaCl (vs from baking soda in Batch 2), Mg from MgCl2 (vs from epsom in Batch 1), and Cl from non-CaCl2 sources. Bru'n input HCO3 = source.Alkalinity × 1.22 = 61 ppm.
brun_target_profile_referenced: User Custom or any — target not used for chemistry parity
